title: The Meeting-Place
content:
혻혻혻혻(A Warning)

혻혻혻혻I saw my fellows
혻혻혻혻In Poverty Street,--
혻혻혻혻Bitter and black with life's defeat,
혻혻혻혻Ill-fed, ill-housed, of ills complete.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I said to myself,--
혻혻혻혻"Surely death were sweet
혻혻혻혻To the people who live in Poverty Street."

혻혻혻혻I saw my fellows
혻혻혻혻In Market Place,--
혻혻혻혻Avid and anxious, and hard of face,
혻혻혻혻Sweating their souls in the Godless race.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I said to myself,--
혻혻혻혻"How shall these find grace
혻혻혻혻Who tread Him to death in the Market Place?"

혻혻혻혻I saw my fellows
혻혻혻혻In Vanity Fair,--
혻혻혻혻Revelling, rollicking, debonair,
혻혻혻혻Life all a Gaudy-Show, never a care.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I said to myself,--
혻혻혻혻"Is there place for these
혻혻혻혻In my Lord's well-appointed policies?"

혻혻혻혻I saw my fellows
혻혻혻혻In Old Church Row,--
혻혻혻혻Hot in discussion of things High and Low,
혻혻혻혻Cold to the seething volcano below.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I said to myself,--
혻혻혻혻"The leaven is dead.
혻혻혻혻The salt has no savour.혻혻혻혻The Spirit is fled."

혻혻혻혻I saw my fellows
혻혻혻혻As men and men,--
혻혻혻혻The Men of Pain, and the Men of Gain,
혻혻혻혻And the Men who lived in Gallanty-Lane.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I said to myself,--
혻혻혻혻"What if those should dare
혻혻혻혻To claim from these others their rightful share?"

혻혻혻혻I saw them all
혻혻혻혻Where the Cross-Roads meet;--
혻혻혻혻Vanity Fair, and Poverty Street,
혻혻혻혻And the Mart, and the Church,--when the Red Drums beat,
혻혻혻혻And summoned them all to The Great Court-Leet.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And I cried unto God,--
혻혻혻혻"Now grant us Thy grace!"

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*

혻혻혻혻For that was a terrible Meeting-Place.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, William Arthur Dunkerley (John Oxenham) delivers a powerful performance in "The Meeting-Place"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
